title: SAVOURY JELLIES OR ASPICS
introduction: Jellies are to cold cookery what consommés and stock are to hot. If anything, the former are perhaps more important, for a cold entrée—however perfect it may be in itself—is nothing without its accompanying jelly.

In the recipes which I give hereafter I have made a point of showing how melting jellies may be obtained, i.e., served in a sauce-boat simultaneously with the cold comestible, or actually poured over it when the latter lies in a deep dish—a common custom nowadays.

This method of serving cold entrées, which I inaugurated at the Savoy Hotel with the “Suprême de Volaille Jeannette,” is the only one which allows of serving a jelly in a state of absolute perfection.

Nevertheless, if a more solid jelly were required, either for the decking of cold dishes or for a moulded entrée, there need only be added to the following formulæ a few gelatine leaves—more or less—according to the required firmness of the jelly.
But it should not be forgotten that the greater the viscosity of the jelly the less value will the same possess.
The various uses of jellies are dealt with in Part II. of this work, where the formulæ of their divers accompanying dishes will also appear.

Stock: 4 lbs (1.8 kg) of strung knuckle of veal
3 lbs (1.4 kg) of strung knuckle of beef
3 lbs (1.4 kg) of veal bones, well broken up
0.5 lb (227 g) of fresh pork rind, well blanched and with fat removed
Clarification: A few leaves of gelatine (optional, if needed for firmness)
2 lbs (907 g) of lean beef, minced then pounded
Flavouring (per quart of aspic): 0.2 pint (95 ml) of liqueur wine (Sherry, Marsala, Madeira, etc.)
OR 0.25 pint (118 ml) of other wine (champagne, hock, etc.)

Stock Preparation: Put the meats in a very clean and well-tinned stockpot or stewpan. Add the cold water, boil, and skim. (Recipe 1)
Having well skimmed the stock, add the salt, put it on the side of the fire, and let it boil gently for four hours.
Remove the meat, taking care not to disturb the stock.
Carefully remove the fat, and garnish with the carrots, onions, leeks, celery, and a large faggot.
Put the whole back on to the fire and cook gently for a further two hours.
Strain through a sieve into a very clean basin and leave to cool.
Clarification of Aspic: When the stock has cooled, remove the grease that has formed on its surface.
Pour the stock off gently into a stewpan of convenient size, preventing the deposit at the bottom of the basin from mixing with the clear liquor.
Test the consistence of the aspic. If it is not firm enough, add a few leaves of gelatine steeped in cold water, being careful not to overdo the quantity.
Add to the stock the lean beef (pounded with the egg white), a little chervil and tarragon, and a few drops of lemon-juice.
Place the saucepan on an open fire, stir its contents with a spatula until the liquid begins to boil.
Remove it from the fire, and place it on the side of the stove, where it may boil gently for half an hour.
Take the saucepan off the fire and remove what little grease has formed on the aspic while cooking.
Strain through a serviette stretched and fastened across the legs of an overturned stool. Repeat the straining process until the aspic becomes quite transparent.
Flavouring the Aspic: Allow the aspic to become quite tepid.
Add the choice wine according to the quantities specified. The wine used should be very clear, free from any deposit, and as perfect as possible in taste.

entries.title: CHICKEN ASPIC
ingredients: 4 lbs (1.8 kg) of strung knuckle of veal
3 lbs (1.4 kg) of strung knuckle of beef
3 lbs (1.4 kg) of veal bones, well broken up
3 calf’s feet, boned and blanched
0.5 lb (227 g) of fresh pork rind, well blanched and with fat removed
2 oven-browned hens, or their equivalent in weight of roasted fowl carcases
Poultry giblets (if handy)
For clarification: Roasted-fowl carcases, well freed from fat
instructions: Prepare the stock with the hens and giblets in the same manner as for ordinary aspic. (Recipe 158)

The clarification follows the same rules as that of the ordinary aspic, except that a few roasted-fowl carcases, previously well freed from fat, are added to the clarification ingredients. (Recipe 158)
notes: In the case of this particularly delicate aspic, it is more than ever necessary not to overdo the amount of gelatine. It should be easily soluble to the palate in order to be perfect.

entries.title: GAME ASPIC
ingredients: Game meats (deer, roebuck, doe, hare, or wild rabbit, previously browned in the oven) to substitute for beef
A few old specimens of feathered game (partridges or pheasants)
For clarification: Meat of the specific game for a defined flavor (e.g., partridge, pheasant, hazel-hen), or available ground game and roast carcases of feathered game for a general flavor
A small quantity of old brandy (optional)
instructions: Prepare this aspic stock in exactly the same way as that of ordinary aspic, substituting the browned game for the beef. (Recipe 158)

The clarification changes according to the different flavours to be given to the aspic. If it is not necessary to give it a special characteristic, prepare it with the meat of that ground game which happens to be most available, adding roast carcases of feathered game.
If the aspic is to have a well-defined flavour, the meat used for the clarification should be that producing the flavour in question (i.e., partridge, pheasant, etc.).
If desired, flavor the aspic with a small quantity of old brandy.
notes: Rather than use an inferior kind of brandy, it is advised to omit it entirely. Without aromatisation the aspic, though imperfect, is passable; but aromatised with bad brandy it is invariably spoilt.

entries.title: FISH ASPIC WITH WHITE WINE
ingredients: Stock ingredients as per fish stock (Recipe 1)
A little saffron (optional)
Up to 8 leaves of gelatine per quart (946 ml) of aspic (if necessary)
Fresh or pressed caviare for clarification
Dry champagne, or a good Bordeaux or Burgundy
4 little crayfish à bisque per quart (946 ml) of aspic (optional, for flavor)
instructions: Prepare the stock in precisely the same manner as fish stock, but the stewpan need not be buttered. (Recipe 1)
If the aspic is not required to be quite white, a little saffron may be added.
When the stock is prepared, test its consistence and rectify, if necessary, with gelatine.
Clarify the stock with fresh or pressed caviare, following the same quantities and procedure as for the clarification of fish consommé. (Recipe 4)
For a special flavor, cook crayfish as for bisque, then pound them, and add them to the fish stock ten minutes before straining. (Recipe 11)
Flavor the aspic by adding the chosen wine only when the aspic is already cold and on the point of coagulating.
notes: The less gelatine is used, the better the aspic will be.
The wine used must be of unquestionably good quality.
Adding the wine when the aspic is cold is the only means of preserving all the aroma of the wine.

entries.title: FISH ASPIC WITH RED WINE
entries.yield: Intended for fish such as trout, salmon, carp, or pike.
ingredients: Court-bouillon with red wine, used for cooking fish (Recipe 165)
Gelatine, as needed
1 egg white, half-whisked, per quart (946 ml) of stock
A few drops of liquid carmine or vegetable red (optional)
instructions: Take the Court-bouillon with red wine that has been used for cooking the fish. (Recipe 165)
Thoroughly remove all grease from the stock. Pour it off carefully, reduce if necessary, and add the required quantity of gelatine. Test for firmness by cooling small quantities in ice.
For clarification, add the half-whisked egg white to the cold stock.
Put the stock over an open fire and stir with a spatula. As soon as it boils, pour the aspic through a serviette fixed on to the legs of an overturned stool.
Repeat the straining process until the required clearness is obtained.
If the wine has lost its color, add a few drops of liquid carmine or vegetable red to achieve a sombre pink color.
notes: The aspic should not be too firm.
The color of red-wine aspic must never be deeper than a sombre pink.
